text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 08 October 2017 

Dr Wang
Saffron Park Hospital

Saffron Park, London, E14 7JL
Dear Dr Wang
John Doe, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: P546484 NHS No. 5484656746
Flat 15 Roundwood Road, London, E14 7JL 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. She works in early-years childcare in a busy, high-noise environment that demands frequent multitasking; she reports that planned midday rest breaks are often interrupted by staffing needs and children’s routines.

Since I last saw her in January 2017, she reports intermittent brief myoclonic jerks, typically in the late morning or shortly after lunch when she has been on her feet and unable to take a quiet break. Her last convulsive seizure was recorded in 10 - 2016, with occasional clusters of jumps persisting. She has adapted by handing over certain tasks when she feels a build-up of warning sensations (described as a sudden internal "jolt" and diffuse unease lasting seconds), though this is not always possible on busy days. There have been no injuries, tongue bites or incontinence. She denies prolonged confusional states; post-event fatigue tends to be mild to moderate.

Medication history: previously tried Lamotrigine up to 100 mg b.d. with partial response but increased dizziness; discontinued in mid-2016. She also trialled Zonisamide to 200 mg nocte in late 2015, stopped due to appetite suppression and low mood. Currently she takes Levetiracetam 1,000 mg b.d. and Clobazam 10 mg nocte, with Propranolol 10 mg t.d.s. as needed for situational tremor/anxiety around observations at work. She reports good adherence (uses a phone alarm and weekly pill organiser). Side effects are limited to occasional irritability and mild morning lethargy, which she feels are manageable.

Sleep is fragmented by early starts; caffeine intake is two cups in the morning and one at midday. Alcohol intake is minimal. She notes that missed or curtailed rest at lunchtime correlates with clusters of brief jerks later in the shift. No new neurological symptoms, headaches or visual disturbances. Menses regular; no pregnancy plans currently.

On examination today, she was alert, oriented and in no distress. Cranial nerves intact, power 5/5 throughout, tone and coordination normal, gait steady. No rashes. Weight stable.

Plan: I have advised she continue her current antiepileptic regimen unchanged for now given the absence of convulsive events over the past year and acceptable tolerability. We discussed practical workplace adjustments she has already initiated, including ensuring a short, uninterrupted midday break where feasible, hydration, and consistent sleep timing; she will liaise with her line manager to formalise these within existing occupational health policies. She is content to proceed without medication changes at present and to keep a diary of any clusters, noting sleep, stress and break interruptions. Routine bloods (U&E, LFTs, FBC) will be checked locally. If clusters increase or new features emerge, we will reconsider options, including adjustment of current therapy. I will review her in six months or sooner if needed.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Her last convulsive seizure was recorded in 10 - 2016, with occasional clusters of jumps persisting.

attributes.Label: multiple cluster per 12 month, multiple per cluster

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Clinic date is 08 October 2017. The letter states ongoing myoclonic seizures: "intermittent brief myoclonic jerks" and specifically mentions "occasional clusters of jumps persisting." This indicates clustered seizure activity with more than one event per cluster, but without a numeric count. For timeframe, the same sentence ties the persistence of clusters to the period after "Her last convulsive seizure was recorded in 10 - 2016," up to the clinic date 08 Oct 2017. Calculate interval: Oct 2016 to Oct 2017 ≈ 12 months. Therefore, within a 12‑month period there have been multiple clusters, with multiple events per cluster. The patient is not seizure free because jerks continue.
seizure_frequency_number: multiple cluster per 12 month, multiple per cluster
